answer:
**"Tazmeen bar sher-e Aneesi Shamlu" (Chapter 24 of *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*) is a poetic elaboration known as a *tazmeen*, a traditional Urdu/Persian form where the poet takes a *sher* (couplet) by another and weaves it into a longer narrative poem, developing its theme.**[^1][^2]

The chapter opens by quoting or alluding to Aneesi Shamlu's couplet (presented in translation as the wandering breeze preferring love's journey over its end), then expands into a dramatic monologue.[^3] The speaker, likened to the lover Qais (Majnun), wanders restlessly until reaching the "land of Pir Sanjar" (likely evoking Sufi mysticism or a shrine). There, a voice from a tomb—representing ancestral Islamic tradition or the sacred *haram*—rebukes him as a "deserter of the ancestral way."[^4] It accuses him of forsaking the inner fire of faith (symbolized by Laila/divine love), failing to cultivate *La Ilaha* (the Islamic declaration of monotheism), and instead building "foreign temples" filled with "church hymns" despite being nurtured in the "House of God" (Kaaba/Bait-Ullah).[^5] The poem culminates by embedding Shamlu's original *sher* (italicized in translation):  
*You learned devotion from us, to serve another's art;*  
*You stole a pearl from our hands, to grace another's heart.*[^6]

This critiques cultural/religious apostasy: Muslims adopting Western/foreign influences while abandoning their roots, framed through Sufi love imagery (Majnun-Laila as metaphor for divine passion). The form is *verse* (as marked in metadata), structured as a *tazmeen* in rhymed, metered lines resembling a *nazm* or extended *ghazal* stanza, with the source *sher* integrated at the close.[^7][^8]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 24 "Tazmeen bar sher-e Aneesi Shamlu" (translation), title and frontmatter ([paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24-tazmeen-bar-sher-e-aneesi-shamlu/translation/#p-e54d95)).

[^2]: Ibid., transliteration variant, title confirms "(Tazmeen)" ([full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24-tazmeen-bar-sher-e-aneesi-shamlu/transliteration/)).

[^3]: Ibid., translation, opening lines ([passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24-tazmeen-bar-sher-e-aneesi-shamlu/translation/#p-33dc81)).

[^4]: Ibid. ([passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24-tazmeen-bar-sher-e-aneesi-shamlu/translation/?p-33dc81#p-33dc81)).

[^5]: Ibid., core rebuke ([passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24-tazmeen-bar-sher-e-aneesi-shamlu/translation/#p-33dc81)).

[^6]: Ibid., closing *sher* ([paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24-tazmeen-bar-sher-e-aneesi-shamlu/translation/#p-ed2ccc)).

[^7]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3* metadata: "layout":"verse" ([full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24-tazmeen-bar-sher-e-aneesi-shamlu/translation/)).

[^8]: Original Urdu confirms poetic structure with rhyme (e.g., *paimaai* / *na-shakeebaai*) ([full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/24-tazmeen-bar-sher-e-aneesi-shamlu/original/)).
